content:
# Luke Chapter 9 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 97-99 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_009_page_001.jpg, LUK_009_page_002.jpg, LUK_009_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And having called together the twelve, he gave them power and authority over all the demons, and to heal diseases.

**2** And he sent them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal the sick.

**3** And he said to them, "Take nothing for the journey, neither staff nor bag nor bread nor money; nor have two tunics apiece.

**4** And into whatever house you enter, remain there, and from there depart.

**5** And as many as do not receive you, when you go out from that city, shake off the dust from your feet as a testimony against them."

**6** And going out, they were passing through village by village, proclaiming the good news and healing everywhere.

**7** Now Herod the tetrarch heard of all that was happening, and he was greatly perplexed, because it was said by some that John had been raised from the dead,

**8** and by some that Elijah had appeared, and by others that a certain prophet of the ancients had risen.

**9** And Herod said, "John I myself beheaded; but who is this about whom I hear such things?" And he was seeking to see him.

**10** And the apostles, having returned, reported to him all that they had done. And he took them and withdrew privately to a city called Bethsaida.

**11** But the crowds, having learned of it, followed him; and having received them, he was speaking to them concerning the kingdom of God, and those who had need of healing he was curing.

**12** And the day began to decline; and the twelve, having come to him, said, "Send the crowd away, so that they may go into the surrounding villages and countryside and find lodging and provisions, for here we are in a desolate place."

**13** But he said to them, "You yourselves give them something to eat." And they said, "We have no more than five loaves and two fish — unless we ourselves should go and buy food for all this people."

**14** For there were about five thousand men. And he said to his disciples, "Make them recline in groups of about fifty each."

**15** And they did so, and made them all recline.

**16** And having taken the five loaves and the two fish, and having looked up to heaven, he blessed them and broke them and was giving them to the disciples to set before the crowd.

**17** And they ate and were all satisfied; and there was taken up what remained to them of the broken pieces, twelve baskets.

**18** And it came to pass, as he was praying alone, the disciples were with him, and he questioned them, saying, "Who do the crowds say that I am?"

**19** And they answered and said, "John the Baptist; but others say Elijah; and others, that a certain prophet of the ancients has risen."

**20** And he said to them, "But who do you say that I am?" And Peter answered and said, "The Christ of God."

**21** And he, having strictly warned them, commanded them to tell this to no one,

**22** saying, "It is necessary that the Son of Man suffer many things and be rejected by the elders and chief priests and scribes, and be killed, and on the third day be raised."

**23** And he was saying to all, "If anyone wishes to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily, and follow me.

**24** For whoever wishes to save his life shall lose it; but whoever loses his life for my sake, this one shall save it.

**25** For what is a man profited, having gained the whole world, but having lost or forfeited himself?

**26** For whoever is ashamed of me and of my words, of this one the Son of Man shall be ashamed when he comes in his glory and that of the Father and of the holy angels.

**27** But I tell you truly, there are some of those standing here who shall not taste death until they see the kingdom of God."

**28** And it came to pass, about eight days after these words, that he took Peter and John and James and went up on the mountain to pray.

**29** And it came to pass, as he was praying, the appearance of his face became different, and his clothing became white, flashing like lightning.

**30** And behold, two men were conversing with him, who were Moses and Elijah,

**31** who, having appeared in glory, were speaking of his departure, which he was about to fulfill in Jerusalem.

**32** Now Peter and those with him were weighed down with sleep; but having fully awakened, they saw his glory and the two men who were standing with him.

**33** And it came to pass, as they were departing from him, Peter said to Jesus, "Master, it is good for us to be here; and let us make three tabernacles, one for you and one for Moses and one for Elijah" — not knowing what he was saying.

**34** And as he was saying these things, a cloud came and was overshadowing them; and they were afraid as they entered into the cloud.

**35** And a voice came out of the cloud, saying, "This is my Son, the Chosen One; listen to him."

**36** And when the voice had come, Jesus was found alone. And they kept silent and reported to no one in those days anything of what they had seen.

**37** And it came to pass, on the next day, when they had come down from the mountain, a great crowd met him.

**38** And behold, a man from the crowd cried out, saying, "Teacher, I beg you to look upon my son, for he is my only-begotten;

**39** and behold, a spirit seizes him, and he suddenly cries out, and it convulses him with foaming, and it departs from him with difficulty, bruising him.

**40** And I begged your disciples to cast it out, and they were not able."

**41** And Jesus answered and said, "O faithless and perverse generation, how long shall I be with you and bear with you? Bring your son here."

**42** And while he was still approaching, the demon threw him down and convulsed him violently. But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit and healed the boy and gave him back to his father.

**43** And all were astonished at the majesty of God. And while all were marveling at all the things which he was doing, he said to his disciples,

**44** "Let these words sink into your ears: for the Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men."

**45** But they did not understand this saying, and it was hidden from them, so that they might not perceive it; and they were afraid to ask him about this saying.

**46** And an argument arose among them as to which of them might be the greatest.

**47** And Jesus, knowing the reasoning of their heart, took hold of a child and set him beside himself,

**48** and said to them, "Whoever receives this child in my name receives me; and whoever receives me receives the one who sent me. For the one who is least among all of you, this one is great."

**49** And John answered and said, "Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we were forbidding him, because he does not follow with us."

**50** And Jesus said to him, "Do not forbid him; for he who is not against you is for you."

**51** And it came to pass, when the days were being fulfilled for his taking up, that he set his face to go to Jerusalem.

**52** And he sent messengers before his face. And they went and entered into a village of the Samaritans, so as to make ready for him.

**53** And they did not receive him, because his face was set to go to Jerusalem.

**54** And when his disciples James and John saw this, they said, "Lord, do you wish that we command fire to come down from heaven and consume them?"

**55** But he turned and rebuked them.

**56** And they went to another village.

**57** And as they were going along the road, someone said to him, "I will follow you wherever you go."

**58** And Jesus said to him, "The foxes have dens, and the birds of heaven have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head."

**59** And he said to another, "Follow me." But he said, "Lord, permit me first to go and bury my father."

**60** And he said to him, "Leave the dead to bury their own dead; but you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God."

**61** And another also said, "I will follow you, Lord; but first permit me to bid farewell to those in my house."

**62** And Jesus said to him, "No one who puts his hand to the plough and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **9:3 — "neither staff" (mete rhabdon):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΗΤΕ ΡΑΒΔΟΝ, prohibiting even a staff. This contrasts with Mark 6:8 which permits one staff — a well-known Synoptic divergence preserved without harmonization.

2. **9:7 — "he was greatly perplexed" (dieporei):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΗΠΟΡΕΙ, the imperfect tense indicating an ongoing state of perplexity. This vivid imperfect captures Herod's sustained bewilderment.

3. **9:10 — "Bethsaida" (Bethsaida):** Sinaiticus clearly reads ΒΗΘΣΑΙΔΑ. Some manuscripts read "Bethsaida" while others omit the city name entirely. The Sinaiticus reading is followed.

4. **9:17 — "twelve baskets" (kophinoi dodeka):** The term ΚΟΦΙΝΟΙ (wicker baskets) is consistently distinguished from ΣΠΥΡΙΔΕΣ (large baskets) used in the feeding of the four thousand, a distinction preserved in formal translation.

5. **9:20 — "The Christ of God" (ton Christon tou Theou):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΝ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ, a distinctly Lukan formulation. The double article construction is rendered formally as "The Christ of God" rather than a less precise "God's Messiah."

6. **9:22 — "be raised" (egerthenai):** The passive infinitive ΕΓΕΡΘΗΝΑΙ ("be raised") is preserved in its passive form, indicating divine agency — God raises the Son of Man — rather than the active "rise."

7. **9:26 — "holy angels" (hagion angelon):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΩΝ ΑΓΙΩΝ ΑΓΓΕΛΩΝ with the adjective preceding the noun. Rendered "holy angels" preserving word order.

8. **9:28 — "about eight days" (hosei hemerai okto):** Luke's "about eight days" differs from Matthew and Mark's "after six days." This is not a contradiction but a different counting convention (inclusive vs. exclusive). The Sinaiticus reading is translated without alteration.

9. **9:29 — "flashing like lightning" (exastrapton):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΞΑΣΤΡΑΠΤΩΝ for the radiance of Christ's garments. This vivid participle, unique to Luke, is rendered with "flashing like lightning" rather than a tamer "dazzling white."

10. **9:31 — "departure" (exodon):** The Greek ΕΞΟΔΟΝ (literally "exodus") is a theologically rich term linking Christ's death to the Exodus of Israel. Rendered "departure" as the formal equivalent, though the resonance with Exodus is noted.

11. **9:33 — "tabernacles" (skenas):** The term ΣΚΗΝΑΣ evokes the Feast of Tabernacles and the wilderness tabernacle. Rendered "tabernacles" rather than "tents" or "shelters" to preserve the liturgical and typological resonance.

12. **9:35 — "the Chosen One" (ho eklelegmenos):** Sinaiticus reads Ο ΕΚΛΕΛΕΓΜΕΝΟΣ ("the Chosen One") rather than the Byzantine Ο ΑΓΑΠΗΤΟΣ ("the Beloved"). This is a significant textual variant; the Sinaiticus reading aligns with Isaiah 42:1 and is preserved as the earlier witness.

13. **9:38 — "only-begotten" (monogenes):** Consistent with the LOGOS rendering at 7:12 and 8:42, ΜΟΝΟΓΕΝΗΣ is translated "only-begotten" to preserve the full weight of this term, applied also to Christ in John 1:14, 18; 3:16, 18.

14. **9:51 — "his taking up" (tes analempseOs autou):** The noun ΑΝΑΛΗΜΨΕΩΣ refers to Christ's ascension but encompasses the entire complex of passion, death, resurrection, and ascension. Rendered "his taking up" as a formal equivalent of this pregnant theological term.

15. **9:54 — Omission of expansion:** Sinaiticus omits the phrase "as Elijah did" (hos kai Elias epoiesen) found in later manuscripts. The shorter Sinaiticus reading is followed, consistent with our text-critical principles.

16. **9:55 — Shorter reading:** Sinaiticus preserves the shorter text, omitting "and said, 'You do not know what manner of spirit you are of'" found in some later witnesses. The shorter reading is maintained.

17. **9:56 — Shorter reading:** Similarly, Sinaiticus omits "For the Son of Man did not come to destroy the lives of men but to save them." This expansion, though theologically sound, is not original to the earliest manuscript tradition.

18. **9:62 — "plough" (arotron):** The agricultural metaphor ΑΡΟΤΡΟΝ is rendered "plough" (British spelling preserved for literary register consistency). The image of looking backward while ploughing powerfully illustrates the undivided commitment required for discipleship.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence maintained throughout:** Each verse preserves Greek word order and syntactic structure where intelligible English permits. Participial constructions are rendered as participles ("having called together," "having taken") rather than being smoothed into finite verb sequences.

2. **Theological vocabulary preserved:** Key terms — Christ (Christos), Son of Man (ho huios tou anthropou), kingdom of God (he basileia tou Theou), the Chosen One (ho eklelegmenos), tabernacles (skenai) — are rendered with established theological vocabulary rather than dynamic equivalents.

3. **Sinaiticus priority:** Where Sinaiticus diverges from the Byzantine majority text, the Sinaiticus reading is followed. This is particularly notable at 9:35 ("the Chosen One" vs. "the Beloved") and in the shorter readings at 9:54-56.

4. **Consistency with Luke 8:** The translation maintains continuity with the preceding chapter in vocabulary choices (e.g., "only-begotten" for monogenes at 9:38 as at 8:42), narrative voice, and register.

5. **Imperfect tenses preserved:** Greek imperfect verbs indicating ongoing or repeated action are rendered with English continuous forms ("was speaking," "was seeking," "were marveling") to preserve the aspectual nuance of the source text.

6. **Discourse markers retained:** Luke's characteristic narrative connectors — "and it came to pass" (egeneto de), "and behold" (kai idou) — are preserved as formal equivalents rather than modernized, maintaining the Semitic-Greek literary quality of the Lukan narrative.
